([/ˌvɛrəˈzɑːnoʊ, -ətˈsɑː-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VERR-ə-ZAH-noh, -⁠ət-SAH-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Italian: [[dʒoˈvanni da (v)verratˈtsaːno]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); often misspelled **Verrazano** in English;[[5]](#cite_note-5) 1491–1528) was an Italian [explorer](/wiki/Exploration "Exploration") from the [Republic of Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) best known for his expedition to [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). He led most of his later missions, including the one to America, in the service of King [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France").

Florentine explorer of North America for France (1485–1528)

"Verrazzano" redirects here. For other uses, see [Verrazzano (disambiguation)](/wiki/Verrazzano_(disambiguation) "Verrazzano (disambiguation)").

In this [Renaissance Italian](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") name, the name *Verrazzano* is an indicator of birthplace or [paternity](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic"), not a [family name](/wiki/Surname "Surname").

**Giovanni da Verrazzano** ([/ˌvɛrəˈzɑːnoʊ, -ətˈsɑː-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*VERR-ə-ZAH-noh, -⁠ət-SAH-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Italian: [[dʒoˈvanni da (v)verratˈtsaːno]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); often misspelled **Verrazano** in English;[[5]](#cite_note-5) 1491–1528) was an Italian [explorer](/wiki/Exploration "Exploration") from the [Republic of Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) best known for his expedition to [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). He led most of his later missions, including the one to America, in the service of King [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France").

He is renowned as the first European to ever explore the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic "Atlantic") coast of North America between [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") in 1524, including [New York Bay](/wiki/New_York_Bay "New York Bay") and [Narragansett Bay](/wiki/Narragansett_Bay "Narragansett Bay").[[8]](#cite_note-Greene1837-8)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_da_Verrazzano&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Verrazzano was born in Val di Greve (now [Greve in Chianti](/wiki/Greve_in_Chianti "Greve in Chianti")), south of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), the capital and main city of the [Republic of Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Recent archival research indicates he was born on July 20, 1491 to Frosino di Lodovico di Cece da Verrazzano and Lisabetta di Leonardo Daffi.[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14) An older hypothesis identified him with a son born in 1485 to Piero Andrea di Bernardo da Verrazzano and Fiammetta Cappelli.

Other theories, now obsolete,[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14) postulated that Verrazzano was born in [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), France, the son of Alessandro di Bartolommeo da Verrazano and Giovanna Guadagni.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) As Ronald S. Love stated, "Verrazzano always considered himself to be Florentine,"[[17]](#cite_note-17) and he was considered a Florentine by his contemporaries as well.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

He signed documents employing a [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") version of his name, "*Janus Verrazanus*",[[note 1]](#cite_note-19) and he called himself "Jehan de Verrazane" in his will dated 11 May 1526 in [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen"), France (preserved at the Archives départementales de la Seine-Maritime).[[19]](#cite_note-20)

In contrast to his detailed account of his voyages to North America, little is definitively known about his personal life. After 1506, he settled in the port of [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe,_Seine-Maritime "Dieppe, Seine-Maritime"), [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), where he began his career as a navigator.[[20]](#cite_note-Morison-21)

He embarked for the American coast probably in 1508 in the company of captain [Thomas Aubert](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Aubert "fr:Thomas Aubert"), on the ship *La Pensée*, equipped by the owner, [Jean Ango](/wiki/Jean_Ango "Jean Ango").[[20]](#cite_note-Morison-21) He explored the region of [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"), possibly during a fishing trip, and possibly the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River") in Canada; on other occasions, he made numerous voyages to the eastern [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## 1522–24 voyage to North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_da_Verrazzano&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1522–24 voyage to North America")]

See also: [Cèllere Codex](/wiki/C%C3%A8llere_Codex "Cèllere Codex")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Viaggioverrazzano.jpg/330px-Viaggioverrazzano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viaggioverrazzano.jpg)

Verrazzano's voyage in 1524

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Carrack_model_La_Dauphine_sailed_by_Verrazzano_1524_Museum_of_NYC.jpg/250px-Carrack_model_La_Dauphine_sailed_by_Verrazzano_1524_Museum_of_NYC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carrack_model_La_Dauphine_sailed_by_Verrazzano_1524_Museum_of_NYC.jpg)

La Dauphine (model) sailed by Verrazzano in 1524

In September 1522, the surviving members of the [Magellan expedition](/wiki/Magellan_expedition "Magellan expedition") returned to Spain, having circumnavigated the globe. Competition in trade was becoming urgent, especially with Portugal.

French merchants and financiers urged King [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") to establish new trade routes. In 1523, the king asked Verrazzano to explore on France's behalf an area between Florida and [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"), intending to find a [sea route to the Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Northwest_Passage "Northwest Passage"). The expedition was funded by a consortium of Florentine merchants based in [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") and [Rouen](/wiki/Rouen "Rouen"), including the Gondi, Rucellai, Nasi, and Albizzi families. Over 20,000 [écus](/wiki/%C3%89cu "Écu") were raised, with Verrazzano himself contributing as both captain and investor.[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14)

Within months, four ships set sail due west for the [Grand Banks of Newfoundland](/wiki/Grand_Banks_of_Newfoundland "Grand Banks of Newfoundland"), but a violent storm and rough seas caused the loss of two ships. The remaining two damaged ships, *[La Dauphine](/wiki/La_Dauphine "La Dauphine")* and *La Normande*, were forced to return to Brittany.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

Repairs were completed in the final weeks of 1523, and the ships set sail again. This time, the ships headed south toward calmer waters under hostile Spanish and Portuguese control.

After a stop in [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), complications forced *La Normande* back to home port, but Verrazzano's ship *La Dauphine* departed on January 17, 1524, piloted by Antoine de Conflans, and headed once more for the North American continent.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

It neared the area of [Cape Fear](/wiki/Cape_Fear_(headland) "Cape Fear (headland)") on March 21, 1524[[24]](#cite_note-25)
and, after a short stay, reached the [Pamlico Sound](/wiki/Pamlico_Sound "Pamlico Sound") lagoon of modern [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"). In a letter to Francis I, described by historians as the *[Cèllere Codex](/wiki/C%C3%A8llere_Codex "Cèllere Codex")*, Verrazzano wrote that he was convinced that the Sound was the beginning of the Pacific Ocean from which access could be gained to China.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

Continuing to explore the coast further northwards, Verrazzano and his crew came into contact with [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") living on the coast. However, he did not notice the entrances to the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay") or the mouth of the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River").[[27]](#cite_note-:0-28)

In [New York Bay](/wiki/New_York_Bay "New York Bay"), he encountered the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") in about 30 [Lenape canoes](/wiki/Lenape_canoes "Lenape canoes") and observed what he deemed to be a large lake, really the entrance to the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). He then sailed along [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") and entered [Narragansett Bay](/wiki/Narragansett_Bay "Narragansett Bay"), where he received a delegation of [Wampanoag](/wiki/Wampanoag_people "Wampanoag people") and [Narragansett people](/wiki/Narragansett_people "Narragansett people").

The words "Norman villa" are found on the 1527 map by [Visconte Maggiolo](/wiki/Visconte_Maggiolo "Visconte Maggiolo") identifying the site. The historian [Samuel Eliot Morison](/wiki/Samuel_Eliot_Morison "Samuel Eliot Morison") writes that "this occurs at [Angouleme (New York)](/wiki/New_Angoul%C3%AAme "New Angoulême") rather than Refugio (Newport). It was probably intended to compliment one of Verrazzano's noble friends. There are several places called 'Normanville' in Normandy, France. The main one is located near [Fécamp](/wiki/F%C3%A9camp "Fécamp") and another important one near [Évreux](/wiki/%C3%89vreux "Évreux"), which would naturally be it. West of it, conjecturally on the Delaware or New Jersey coast, is a *Longa Villa*, which Verrazzano certainly named after [François d'Orléans, duc de Longueville](/wiki/Duke_of_Longueville "Duke of Longueville")."[[27]](#cite_note-:0-28) He stayed there for two weeks and then moved northwards.[[28]](#cite_note-:1-29)

He discovered [Cape Cod Bay](/wiki/Cape_Cod_Bay "Cape Cod Bay"), his claim being proved by a map of 1529 that clearly outlined Cape Cod.[[28]](#cite_note-:1-29) He named the cape after a general, calling it Pallavicino.[[29]](#cite_note-30) He then followed the coast up to modern [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), southeastern [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"), and he then returned to France by 8 July 1524. Verrazzano named the region that he explored *Francesca* in honour of the French king, but his brother's map labelled it *Nova Gallia* ([New France](/wiki/New_France "New France")).[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Later life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_da_Verrazzano&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later life and death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Stemma_di_Giovanni_da_Verrazzano_Coat_of_arms.svg/250px-Stemma_di_Giovanni_da_Verrazzano_Coat_of_arms.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Stemma_di_Giovanni_da_Verrazzano_Coat_of_arms.svg)

Coat of arms of Giovanni da Verrazzano

Verrazzano arranged a second voyage, with financial support from [Jean Ango](/wiki/Jean_Ango "Jean Ango") and [Philippe de Chabot](/wiki/Philippe_de_Chabot "Philippe de Chabot"), which departed from Dieppe with four ships early in 1527. One ship was separated from the others in a gale near the [Cape Verde Islands](/wiki/Cape_Verde_Islands "Cape Verde Islands"). Still, Verrazzano reached the coast of Brazil with two ships and harvested a cargo of [brazilwood](/wiki/Brazilwood "Brazilwood") before returning to Dieppe in September. The third ship returned later, also with a cargo of brazilwood.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The partial success did not find the desired passage to the Pacific Ocean, but it inspired Verrazzano's final voyage, which left Dieppe in early 1528.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

There are conflicting accounts of Verrazzano's demise. In one version, during his third voyage to North America in 1528, after he had explored Florida, [the Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"), and the [Lesser Antilles](/wiki/Lesser_Antilles "Lesser Antilles"), Verrazzano anchored out to sea and rowed ashore, probably on the island of [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe"). He was allegedly [killed and eaten](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") by the native [Caribs](/wiki/Island_Caribs "Island Caribs").[[33]](#cite_note-34) The fleet of two or three ships was anchored out of gunshot range, and no one could respond in time.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Some earlier historians hypothesized that Verrazzano was the same person as the corsair [Jean Fleury](/wiki/Jean_Fleury "Jean Fleury"), who was executed for piracy by the Spanish.[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) This theory has been definitively rejected by historians, notably [Prospero Peragallo](/w/index.php?title=Prospero_Peragallo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prospero Peragallo (page does not exist)"), who demonstrated that the two were distinct individuals.[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14) After evaluating the evidence for the various versions of Verrazzano's death, Lawrence C. Wroth concluded that his cannibalization by Caribs may well be true, while the hypothesis that he was hanged as Fleury is highly implausible.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWroth1970255–262-38)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_da_Verrazzano&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/1527-TeraFlorida.jpg/250px-1527-TeraFlorida.jpg)](/wiki/File:1527-TeraFlorida.jpg)

1527 map by [Visconte Maggiolo](/wiki/Visconte_Maggiolo "Visconte Maggiolo") showing the east coast of North America with "Tera Florida" at top right and "Lavoradore" (Labrador) at bottom left. The information supposedly[[38]](#cite_note-39) came from Giovanni da Verrazzano's voyage in 1524. ([Biblioteca Ambrosiana](/wiki/Biblioteca_Ambrosiana "Biblioteca Ambrosiana") Milan.)

The geographic information derived from this voyage significantly influenced sixteenth-century cartographers.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Despite his discoveries, Verrazzano's reputation did not proliferate as much as other explorers of that era. For example, Verrazzano gave the European name Francesca to the new land that he had seen, in accordance with contemporary practices, after the French king in whose name he sailed. That and other names he bestowed on features he discovered have not survived. He had the misfortune of making significant discoveries shortly after the years (1519 to 1521) that the dramatic [Conquest of the Aztec Empire](/wiki/Conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Conquest of the Aztec Empire") and the first circumnavigation of the world occurred.

In the 19th and early 20th centuries, there was a great debate in the United States about the authenticity of the letters that Verrazzano ostensibly wrote to Francis I to describe the geography, flora, fauna, and native population of the east coast of North America.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Others thought that they were authentic, in particular since the discovery of the [Cèllere Codex](/wiki/C%C3%A8llere_Codex "Cèllere Codex") in 1909. This is the most widely held opinion nowadays,[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWroth19703–5-42) particularly after the discovery of a letter signed by Francis I, which referred to Verrazzano's letter.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Verrazzano's reputation was particularly obscure in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), where the 1609 voyage of [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson") on behalf of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") came to be regarded as the *de facto* start of European exploration of New York ([Estêvão Gomes](/wiki/Est%C3%AAv%C3%A3o_Gomes "Estêvão Gomes")'s trip of 1524 was likewise forgotten). It was only by a great effort by the [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") community in 1909 [[14]](#cite_note-:2-14) and then in the 1940s and 1950s that Verrazzano's name and reputation were established as the European discoverer of the harbour, culminating in [an initiative to name](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge#Naming "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") the newly built [Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") after him.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

* In 1909, during the [Hudson-Fulton Celebration](/wiki/Hudson-Fulton_Celebration "Hudson-Fulton Celebration"), a bronze statue of Verrazzano by [Ettore Ximenes](/wiki/Ettore_Ximenes "Ettore Ximenes") was installed in [Battery Park](/wiki/The_Battery_(Manhattan) "The Battery (Manhattan)") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").[[44]](#cite_note-45)
* There are numerous commemorations of the explorer on [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island").
  + The [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge"), spanning [The Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows") that separate Staten Island from [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), is perhaps the best known. Until October 2018, it was known as the "Verrazano–Narrows Bridge" with one "z".[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)
  + A [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry") boat that served New York from the 1950s to the 1990s was also named for Verrazzano. The ferry was named the "Verrazzano", while the bridge was named "Verrazano".[[47]](#cite_note-48)
  + A [Little League](/wiki/Little_League "Little League") team on Staten Island is also named for him.[[48]](#cite_note-49)
* The [Jamestown Verrazzano Bridge](/wiki/Jamestown_Verrazzano_Bridge "Jamestown Verrazzano Bridge") in [Narragansett Bay](/wiki/Narragansett_Bay "Narragansett Bay"), Rhode Island, is named for him, as is Maryland's [Verrazano Bridge](/wiki/Verrazano_Bridge_(Maryland) "Verrazano Bridge (Maryland)").[[49]](#cite_note-50)
* [A vessel](/wiki/Italian_destroyer_Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Italian destroyer Giovanni da Verrazzano") of the [Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina"), a destroyer of the [*Navigatori* class](/wiki/Navigatori-class_destroyer "Navigatori-class destroyer"), was named after Verrazzano. She was launched in 1930 and sunk by a British [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") in 1942.
* There is a statue of him in the town of [Greve in Chianti](/wiki/Greve_in_Chianti "Greve in Chianti"), Italy.[[50]](#cite_note-51)
* There is a monument commemorating him in [Rehoboth Beach, Delaware](/wiki/Rehoboth_Beach,_Delaware "Rehoboth Beach, Delaware"); it states on its south face:

> In Commemoration of  
> Verrazzano's  
> Voyage to  
> America  
> erected by the  
> Delaware Commission on  
> Italian Heritage and Culture  
> 2008[[51]](#cite_note-Giovanni_Da_Verrazzano_Historical_Marker-52)

:   The monument further states on its east face:

> A native of Val Di Greve in the Tuscany region of Italy, he studied navigation as a young man and became a master mariner. He was engaged by the King of France to lead a voyage to North America in 1524. The purpose of Verrazzano's journey was to learn more about the continent. Traveling in a small ship known as the Dauphine, he explored coastal areas from the present-day State of North Carolina to Canada, observing the natural abundance of the land and the vibrant culture of its native peoples. His voyage is the earliest documented European exploration of this part of the Atlantic Coast.
>
> This monument rests upon stone from Castello di Verrazzano, the explorer's ancestral home.[[51]](#cite_note-Giovanni_Da_Verrazzano_Historical_Marker-52)
